persona.name: David Park
persona.role: Staff attorney at a consumer rights organization
persona.core_preferences: Authoritative but accessible to non-lawyers. Evidence-based arguments with clear recommendations. Structured with headers for skimming.
persona.hidden_preferences: The opening paragraph should make the reader care about the HUMAN IMPACT before introducing any legal framework — leading with statute numbers or regulatory citations loses everyone who isn't already an expert. Each argument section should follow a pattern: state the principle, give the real-world example that makes it concrete, then connect it to the specific policy recommendation — arguments without concrete examples feel academic, examples without principles feel anecdotal. Technical legal language should be used precisely but always followed by a plain-language restatement in the same sentence or the next — never assume the reader knows what 'preemption' or 'strict liability' means without context. Recommendations should be graduated — a maximalist position plus a fallback — because policymakers need to see what's ideal AND what's acceptable; all-or-nothing asks get ignored. The tone should convey urgency without alarmism — the difference between 'consumers are currently unprotected' and 'this is a crisis'; overstating the problem undermines credibility with sophisticated readers.
persona.dealbreakers: Partisan framing that reads as advocacy rather than analysis, unsupported legal claims, burying the recommendation at the end instead of leading with it, using emotional language where evidence should speak
persona.initial_task: Help me draft the opening argument (2-3 paragraphs) of a public comment on the FTC's proposed rule about subscription cancellation — we want to argue that the current 'click to cancel' requirement doesn't go far enough for consumers.
tasks: Help me draft the opening argument (2-3 paragraphs) of a public comment on the FTC's proposed rule about subscription cancellation — we want to argue that the current 'click to cancel' requirement doesn't go far enough for consumers.

I need to draft the core argument section (2-3 paragraphs) of a policy brief on data broker regulation — we want to argue that the current opt-out model puts an unreasonable burden on consumers and that the FTC should require opt-in consent. A woman in Ohio had her home address sold to a stalker through a data broker and the current law gave her no recourse.

Help me write the opening section (2-3 paragraphs) of a public comment on the CFPB's proposed rule about earned wage access products — we want to argue that these are effectively loans and should be regulated as such, but we also need to acknowledge that an outright ban would hurt workers who depend on them.

I need to draft the key argument (2-3 paragraphs) of a policy brief on algorithmic pricing in rental housing — landlords are using revenue management software that coordinates pricing across properties, and we want to argue this constitutes a form of price-fixing that harms tenants. A family in Atlanta saw their rent increase 40% in one year after their building switched to algorithmic pricing.

Help me write the central argument section (2-3 paragraphs) for a public comment on the FCC's proposed rules around broadband nutrition labels — we support the disclosure requirement but want to argue the proposed format doesn't go far enough because it allows ISPs to bury fees in footnotes. A senior in rural Michigan signed up for a $30/month plan that actually cost $67 after hidden fees.
